artifacts.content:
From: Michael Delgado <m.delgado@frontrangelogic.com>
To: Intake Coordinator <intake@auroracounselingcenter.org>
Date: 2025-02-18 09:42:11 -0700
Subject: New Client Paperwork Follow-Up – Michael Delgado

Hi Jenna,

Thanks for sending over the intake forms yesterday. I went through everything this morning before my first stand-up and wanted to confirm a couple of items so your records are accurate.

On the “Household and Relationships” page, there’s a field asking how many times I’ve been divorced. I entered 0.0 there, since Emily and I have been married for almost 11 years and neither of us has been married before.

For the “What brings you in” section, I wrote about ongoing anxiety, trouble sleeping, and feeling burned out with work and parenting. I also added some context that I’m a senior project manager at a software company and have been struggling with chest tightness and racing heart episodes that urgent care and my PCP say are stress-related.

I wasn’t sure how much detail to include about alcohol, but I did note that since around Labor Day 2024 I’ve been drinking 3–4 IPAs most weeknights, and sometimes up to 6 on Fridays. It’s usually on the couch after the kids go down around 8:30 p.m. My wife confronted me about it on December 2, 2024, after she saw I’d spent $186 in one month at Mile High Liquors on Colfax. I’m not blacking out, but I’m clearly using beer to numb out anxiety and dread about work.

Please let me know if I should revise anything before my first appointment on March 4.

Best,
Michael

Michael Aaron Delgado
Senior Project Manager, FrontRange Logic, Inc.
m.delgado@frontrangelogic.com | 303-555-0194

metadata.subject: New Client Paperwork Follow-Up – Michael Delgado

artifacts.content:
From: Emily Delgado <emily.delgado@example.com>
To: Michael Delgado <m.delgado@frontrangelogic.com>
Date: March 4, 2025 9:12 PM
Subject: Please, can we talk like adults tomorrow?

Mike,

I know we’re both exhausted, but I don’t want us to keep snapping at each other and then pretending it didn’t happen. Tonight’s argument about your mom watching the kids was not really about your mom.

You said something that really stuck with me: that you’re scared we’re “headed for divorce number two” if we don’t figure this out. I don’t think throwing around phrases like “second divorce” in front of the dishwasher helps either of us calm down. I married you because I believed we could do hard things together, not because I thought we’d repeat what happened with you and Jenna.

I also want you to remember how chaotic it was when your cousin Elena and little Mateo were sleeping on our couch from January to March 2023 after she left her ex. We got through that as a team, even with my mom, Patricia, basically hiding out in our guest room last May after that huge fight with your father‑in‑law about his gambling. If we could handle all those extra people in this tiny townhouse, we can handle our own communication.

Can we set aside 30 minutes tomorrow night after the kids are in bed—no phones, no TV—and just talk about what we each need? I’m not interested in keeping score about who threatens divorce more; I’m interested in us feeling like partners again.

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: Please, can we talk like adults tomorrow?

contains_negative_info: mentions marital conflict and threat of "second divorce"
mentions gambling debt and family hiding from spouse

artifacts.content:
Title: Mediation Consult – post-divorce parenting
Date: June 18, 2024
Time: 2:30 PM – 3:30 PM
Location: Zoom (link in confirmation email)
Attendees: Michael Delgado; Jenna Ruiz; Atty. Laura Feld (mediator)

Description:

Initial consult with family law mediator re: lingering issues from first divorce agreement with Jenna. Agenda:

1. Clarify remaining obligations from 2012 separation and 2013 finalized divorce decree, especially around old joint credit card and the small personal loan Michael co‑signed.
2. Discuss whether it makes sense to formally modify the parenting plan on paper, even though in practice Michael hasn’t seen Jenna’s daughter in years and there are no ongoing visitation disputes.
3. Ask Laura whether the fact that Michael is now remarried with two kids in Aurora changes anything about his legal exposure from the first marriage.
4. Confirm that the prior dissolution of marriage is fully closed out in Colorado courts so it doesn’t complicate any future estate planning with Emily.

Notes Michael jotted before the call:
- Emphasize that this is about tying up loose ends from divorce #1, not reopening emotional stuff.
- Mention that day to day it’s just me, Emily, and the kids at home, though my mother‑in‑law Patricia stayed with us May 1–21, 2024 while she was talking to a lawyer in Minnesota about separating from Mark over his gambling.
- Ask if any of this history could impact background checks for client work at FrontRange Logic.
contains_negative_info: mentions prior divorce and legal/financial issues
mentions gambling and marital separation in extended family

artifacts.content:
From: HR Notifications <hr@frontrangelogic.com>
To: Michael Delgado <m.delgado@frontrangelogic.com>
Date: October 6, 2023 11:04 AM
Subject: Follow-up re: workload accommodation discussion

Hi Michael,

Thank you for meeting with Kevin and Lisa on October 3 to discuss your workload and the medical documentation you provided. As a recap, you shared the cardiology consult from UCHealth dated September 29, 2023, indicating that your chest pain is likely stress‑related.

Per our conversation, Kevin has reassigned one smaller client implementation to another PM. However, your two major enterprise projects will remain under your leadership at this time. Lisa raised the question of whether your health concerns, combined with the ongoing stress of managing a blended family and a prior divorce, might warrant a temporary reduction in hours, but you indicated you preferred to maintain full‑time status.

We recognize that juggling responsibilities as a senior project manager, co‑parenting with an ex‑spouse from your first marriage, and supporting your current household (Emily, the kids, and occasional extended family houseguests like your cousin Elena and her son Mateo earlier this year) can be challenging. Please remember that the Employee Assistance Program offers short‑term counseling, including for relationship stress related to divorce or remarriage.

As discussed, we will:
- Reevaluate your workload in 60 days.
- Encourage you to use available PTO for medical and personal appointments.
- Revisit whether any additional accommodations are necessary if your cardiologist recommends further changes.

This email is for documentation purposes only and does not alter your at‑will employment status.

Best regards,

Maya Chen
HR Business Partner
FrontRange Logic, Inc.
contains_negative_info: mentions stress-related medical issues and workload concerns
mentions prior divorce and relationship stress

artifacts.content:
Title: Coffee w/ Daniel – talk about Dad & marriages
Date: November 11, 2024
Time: 9:00 AM – 10:15 AM
Location: Stella’s Coffee, Pearl St.
Attendees: Michael Delgado; Daniel Delgado

Description:

Catch‑up with Daniel while he’s down from Fort Collins for the long weekend.

Talking points (Michael’s notes):
- Check in on how he’s really doing after his breakup this summer; he keeps joking he “dodged divorce number one,” but he seemed shaken.
- Share my own worries about how the stress from my first divorce still shows up in little ways—like how fast I go to worst‑case thinking when Emily and I argue.
- Ask Daniel if he ever felt like Mom and Dad were close to splitting up before Dad’s heart attack, or if that’s just me projecting.
- Update him on home front: Emily working more shifts, kids doing okay, house feeling crowded again when my mother‑in‑law Patricia crashed with us May 1–21 after walking out on Mark over his gambling. Plus the memory of when Elena and Mateo stayed with us for those months in early 2023 after she fled her ex.
- Talk honestly about what it’s like to be someone who’s already been divorced once and is terrified of repeating the pattern. I keep thinking, “I survived one failed marriage; I can’t screw this one up too.”

Reminder: bring Dad’s old watch to give to Daniel, per Dad’s request.

metadata.title: Coffee w/ Daniel – talk about Dad & marriages

contains_negative_info: mentions prior divorce and fear of marital failure
mentions gambling and family walking out

artifacts.content:
From: Atty. Rebecca Nolan <rnolan@denverfamilylawgroup.com>
To: Michael Delgado <m.delgado.personal@gmail.com>
Date: May 22, 2024 3:47 PM
Subject: Clarification re: prior dissolution of marriage

Dear Mr. Delgado,

Following up on our brief phone consultation, I am writing to summarize my understanding of your situation and to clarify how your prior dissolution of marriage may intersect with current family dynamics.

You indicated that you were previously divorced in your early 20s, after a short first marriage that did not involve children or real property. My review of the 2013 decree suggests there are no outstanding legal obligations from that divorce, and it should not directly impact your current marriage to Emily or your parental rights regarding your two children.

However, you also mentioned heightened anxiety about “ending up divorced twice,” particularly when conflict arises at home or when extended family members’ relationships are in turmoil. You referenced your cousin Elena staying with you (along with her son Mateo) from January to March 2023 after leaving an abusive partner, and your mother‑in‑law Patricia living in your guest room from May 1–21, 2024 while she consulted a Minnesota attorney about separating from her husband due to gambling debts.

From a legal standpoint, none of these situations change the finality of your first divorce. If you and Emily ever pursued marital counseling or, in a worst‑case scenario, a legal separation, it would be treated as a separate, second matter.

Please note that this email does not constitute a full attorney‑client engagement and is for informational purposes only.

Sincerely,

Rebecca Nolan
Denver Family Law Group

metadata.subject: Clarification re: prior dissolution of marriage

contains_negative_info: mentions prior divorce and fear of second divorce
mentions abusive relationship and gambling debts in family

artifacts.content:
From: Emily Delgado <emily.delgado@example.com>
To: Michael Delgado <michael.delgado@frontrangelogic.com>
Date: May 3, 2025 9:18 PM
Subject: Pastor Miller’s questionnaire

Hey Mike,

Can you take a look at the premarital counseling questionnaire Pastor Miller sent for Mark and Jenna and see if it looks familiar? He said since we’ve “been through this twice between the two of us,” he figured we’d have thoughts.

One of the questions asks how many times each person has been married and divorced, and I immediately thought about how awkward that was for you back when we were doing the joint session with my mom. You handled talking about your first marriage way better than I did talking about my parents splitting up.

I told Jenna that it’s not a big deal to say she’s never been divorced and that you only went through it once in your late twenties, but she’s stressing that it’s going to make Mark’s parents judge her. Honestly, I think they’re just trying to make sure nobody’s hiding a second or third divorce or anything like that.

Also, random, but since you’re already in questionnaire mode: did you ever follow up with that MindWay Clinic nurse practitioner, Sarah Klein, after she put that April 12, 2024 video check‑in on your calendar? You mentioned you started on 25 mg of sertraline and then got spooked about the MetLife life insurance screening. Just wondering if you ever circled back or if that whole thing is still hanging over you.

Anyway, can you skim the attachment before bed and tell me if there’s anything you wish they’d asked us back then?

Love,
Emily

From: Daniel Delgado <daniel.delgado@fcschools.org>
To: Michael Delgado <michael.delgado@frontrangelogic.com>
Date: February 10, 2025 7:41 AM
Subject: That relationship survey you sent

Yo big bro,

Finally got around to that relationship satisfaction survey you forwarded from your therapist. Some of those questions were intense.

The one that jumped out at me was where it asked how many serious relationships you’ve had that ended in divorce or separation. I remember you telling me after your first divorce that you never wanted to see that word on a form again, so it’s wild they make you list it out like it’s a parking ticket. I checked “never married,” obviously, but I kept thinking about you having to put “1” in that little box. No wonder you hate filling out this stuff for insurance and HR.

The section about coping really hit home too. When it asked what you’ve tried to manage stress, I almost wrote, “Not stealing meds from family,” because I still feel guilty about that night you called me after taking Mom’s leftover lorazepam. You said you took 0.5 mg around midnight at the Lakewood house, didn’t tell Emily, and just needed your brain to shut up about work. I know you said you only did it that once, but man, that scared me.

Anyway, if the survey is supposed to help you and Emily figure out patterns, I’d say it’s doing its job. Just remember that having one divorce in your history doesn’t define you.

Talk soon,
Dan

Title: Intake – Couples Counseling (History & Prior Marriages)
Date: June 6, 2025 4:00–5:00 PM
Location: Telehealth – Aurora Family Therapy Center (Zoom)
Attendees: Michael Delgado; Emily Delgado; Dr. Andrea Holt

Description:

Initial couples counseling intake with Dr. Holt. Focus on relationship history, prior marriages, and family patterns.

Notes to self before session:
- Be honest about my previous marriage in my late twenties and how fast we rushed into it after only 9 months of dating.
- Be prepared to answer the inevitable “How many times have you been married and divorced?” question without getting defensive or minimizing it as “just one quick mistake.”
- Emily wants to talk about how my experience with that divorce still shows up when we argue (e.g., me shutting down because I’m terrified of repeating that separation).
- Dr. Holt’s pre‑intake form already asked me to list dates of marriage and divorce; I left the exact dates blank because I couldn’t remember, but I did indicate that I’ve gone through divorce once.
- She also asked what I’ve tried for anxiety: need to mention the MindWay Clinic thing last March (video intake with Sarah Klein, 25 mg sertraline for three weeks, then I bailed because of the MetLife life insurance screening).

Goals for session:
- Explain how juggling work stress, the kids, and the baggage from my first divorce is affecting our current marriage.
- Figure out how to talk about my past without Emily feeling like she’s competing with a ghost.
- Start a plan for better communication before conflicts escalate.

From: HR Benefits <benefits@frontrangelogic.com>
To: Michael Delgado <michael.delgado@frontrangelogic.com>
Date: October 21, 2024 2:06 PM
Subject: MetLife Life Insurance – Additional Information Needed

Dear Michael,

As part of your recent application to increase supplemental life insurance coverage through MetLife, we require clarification on a few items in your submitted questionnaire.

1. Marital History
On the form, you checked the box indicating you are currently married and have been divorced once previously. Please confirm that this information is accurate and that there are no additional prior marriages or divorces that should be disclosed. MetLife’s underwriting guidelines require a complete marital history, including the total number of marriages and the number that ended in divorce.

2. Medical and Behavioral Health History
You noted experiencing work‑related stress and intermittent anxiety over the last two years. You also indicated that you recently consulted an online psychiatry provider but left the medication section blank. If you have started, changed, or stopped any prescribed medications (for example, sertraline or similar antidepressants) within the past 12 months, please provide the name, dose, and dates. This includes treatment received via telehealth services such as MindWay Clinic.

Please respond to this email with the requested details or upload an updated medical history form via the Benefits Portal by October 31, 2024, so we can continue processing your application.

If you have any questions, feel free to contact the Benefits Team at extension 234.

Sincerely,

Laura Chen
HR & Benefits Specialist
FrontRange Logic, Inc.

Full name: Michael Aaron Delgado
DOB: 08/14/1986
Best contact number: (303) 555-8427 (cell – text is usually fine unless I’m in meetings)

I’ll fill out the full history forms tonight, but I thought it might be helpful to give a quick snapshot of what’s going on. My anxiety really escalated after an informal performance discussion with my VP, Jason Miller, in early October 2024 in his office at FrontRange Logic. He told me there had been “concerns” about my responsiveness and that if I couldn’t handle multiple concurrent projects, they might need to “revisit my role” in Q1 2025.

Since that conversation, I’ve been obsessively checking email until 1–2 a.m. most nights and waking up around 5:30 a.m. to get a jump on Slack messages. I’m constantly worried I’m going to be put on a performance improvement plan or laid off, and it’s making it hard to focus at work or be present with my family.

I work downtown on Tuesdays and Thursdays but can usually step out for a telehealth session from my car or a conference room if needed. If anything opens up earlier than the 21st, please feel free to call or text my cell.

Michael

Hey Em,

From: Michael Delgado <m.delgado@frontrangelogic.com>
To: Dr. Harris <intake@auroracounselingcenter.org>
Date: 2025-02-11 08:17
Subject: New Client Intake Forms + Address Confirmation

Hi Dr. Harris,

I’m sending over the intake paperwork you requested before our first session on Thursday. I filled everything out in the PDF, but I wanted to double‑check that my contact info came through clearly in case the form fields didn’t save.

Name: Michael Aaron Delgado  
Cell: 303-555-1842  
Home address: 7429 Willow Bend Drive, Aurora, CO 80015

For the “How long has this been occurring?” question, I tried to give a complete answer in the box, but here’s a bit more context since there wasn’t much room. The really bad stretch has been going on for about 6 months, basically since the night of October 3, 2024, when I ended up in the ER at Denver Health with crushing chest pain and a 140+ heart rate. They ran bloodwork and an EKG, ruled out a heart attack, and coded it as a “panic attack” on the discharge paperwork. Ever since that ER visit, I’ve had these episodes at least 3–4 times a week, especially when I’m driving home on I‑25 from the FrontRange office.

I’m still functioning at work and at home, but it feels like I’m constantly bracing for the next episode, which is why I finally reached out.

Please let me know if you need a photo of my ID or insurance card before Thursday, or if anything about the address or contact info above doesn’t match what you have on file.

Thanks,

Michael
Senior Project Manager, FrontRange Logic, Inc.
Aurora, CO

metadata.subject: New Client Intake Forms + Address Confirmation

From: Michael Delgado <m.delgado@frontrangelogic.com>
To: HR Benefits <benefits@frontrangelogic.com>
Date: January 29, 2025 9:14 AM
Subject: Address verification for EAP paperwork

Hi Jenna,

I’m filling out the Employee Assistance Program intake packet the ER social worker pointed me to after that anxiety episode I had on January 18 during the status meeting with Melissa Grant. That was the one where my chest got so tight they called an ambulance and sent me over to Denver Health Medical Center, and Dr. Ravi Patel strongly suggested I follow up with a therapist.

The EAP portal is asking me to confirm my “current residential address on file with employer.” When I log into Workday, it still shows our old apartment:

    1183 S. Jamaica Street, Apt 304
    Aurora, CO 80012

We moved to the townhouse near Cherry Creek State Park about five years ago, so that’s obviously wrong, but I don’t want to mess up the insurance side of things by editing it incorrectly. Can you confirm what format you need for the new address and whether I should update it directly in Workday or send it through you first?

The packet also asks who lives with me; I noted that it’s me, my wife Emily, and our two kids, plus that my brother Daniel stayed with us March–June 2024 while doing his 90 days of outpatient treatment at Aurora Recovery Center after his second DUI.

Once I know the correct way to submit the updated address, I’ll finish the forms.

Thanks,
Michael

From: Emily Delgado <emily.delgado@gmail.com>
To: Michael Delgado <m.delgado@frontrangelogic.com>
Date: November 12, 2024 7:58 PM
Subject: Mortgage statement + address mix-up

Mike,

Just a heads-up that the mortgage company still has our mailing address listed as the old rental on the latest statement:

    1183 S. Jamaica Street, Apt 304
    Aurora, CO 80012

I thought we fixed this when we closed on the townhouse, but apparently not. Can you call them tomorrow between meetings and get it changed to the current place? I don’t want important stuff going to that mailbox.

Also, I’m still really shaken after finding the $7,800 balance on the Chase Visa this weekend. You said some of it was work-travel expenses and random Amazon things, but it feels like the same pattern as when you took out that $12,000 personal loan through Wells Fargo downtown back in March 2023 for the credit cards and the NBA betting. I spent all of Saturday and Sunday in our kitchen feeling like I don’t know what else might be hidden.

If we’re going to keep sharing accounts and an address, I need to be able to trust that you’re telling me the full picture. When you’re on your phone checking scores or “our accounts,” I’m bracing for another surprise.

Please call the mortgage company tomorrow and send me a quick note once the address is fixed.

– E

artifacts.content:
From: HR Onboarding <hr@denverbehavioralwellness.org>
To: Michael Delgado <m.delgado@gmail.com>
Date: 2023-01-05 09:18 AM MST
Subject: Intake Forms – Please Complete Before First Group Session

Hi Michael,

We’re looking forward to having you in the 6‑week online CBT group for anxiety starting Tuesday, January 10, 7–8 p.m. Before your first session, please complete the attached intake packet.

A few fields have been left blank on what you already submitted via the portal:

1. Date of Birth
   You listed your age as 37, but the actual date field is empty. Please enter your full DOB in the format MM/DD/YYYY so we can match your record with your insurance profile.

2. Emergency Contact
   You listed Emily as your emergency contact but didn’t include her phone number. Please add that as well.

3. Medical/Stress History
   You briefly mentioned a “near-panic episode during a sprint review at work in December” and that you’ve been checking your blood pressure frequently. If you’re comfortable, please add the approximate month and year when your symptoms started and any major life events you believe contributed.

For example, some clients note things like a family member’s heart attack or a stressful work incident. Context like your dad’s minor heart attack in November 2023 or the written warning from your director at FrontRange Logic could be helpful for your group therapist to understand the timeline, but specific dates of those events are optional. The only date we absolutely need is your date of birth.

Please email the updated forms back or upload them to the portal by Monday at 5 p.m.

Best,
Megan
Intake Coordinator, Denver Behavioral Wellness

From: Emily Delgado <emily.delgado@gmail.com>
To: Michael Delgado <m.delgado@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-04-10 07:42 PM MDT
Subject: Birthday stuff – please read :)

Hey,

I just got the reminder from Google Photos that it’s almost your “Colorado spring birthday” again, which means I need to figure out what we’re doing this year. I know you keep saying you don’t want to make a big deal out of turning 39, but the kids are excited and already talking about cake.

A few logistics:

1. I booked the sitter for Friday the 19th so we can do a low‑key dinner just the two of us the weekend after your actual birthday. That way your parents can come over on the Sunday before for the family thing without everything piling into one day.

2. Sophie wants to make you a guitar‑shaped cake (pray for us). Can you secretly tell me what kind of frosting you like this year so I can “suggest” it to her?

3. I’m putting together a little memory book with photos from when you were a baby through now. Your mom keeps sending me pictures of you as a toddler in that ridiculous Broncos onesie and keeps reminding me that you were born “during a nasty April snowstorm.” She couldn’t remember the exact date at first, just that it was mid‑April and she had to dig the car out to get to the hospital.

Also, I know work has been rough since that written warning from Jason last August, but I really want your birthday weekend to feel like a break from all that.

Love you,
Em

Since then you’ve been working 55–60 hr weeks and checking email until midnight most nights because you’re terrified of losing the job and not being able to cover the $2,150 mortgage.
